lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Where hast thou been, sister?

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Killing swine.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Sister, where thou?

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: A sailor’s wife had chestnuts in her lap,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: And munch’d, and munch’d, and munch’d:—“Give me,” quoth I:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: “Aroint thee, witch!” the rump-fed ronyon cries.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Her husband’s to Aleppo gone, master o’ the Tiger:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: But in a sieve I’ll thither sail,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: And, like a rat without a tail,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: I’ll do, I’ll do, and I’ll do.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: I’ll give thee a wind.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Thou’rt kind.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: And I another.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: I myself have all the other,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: And the very ports they blow,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: All the quarters that they know

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: I’ the shipman’s card.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: I will drain him dry as hay:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Sleep shall neither night nor day

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Hang upon his pent-house lid;

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: He shall live a man forbid:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Weary sennights nine times nine

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Shall he dwindle, peak and pine:

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Though his bark cannot be lost,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Yet it shall be tempest-tost.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Look what I have.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Show me, show me.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Here I have a pilot’s thumb,

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Wreck’d as homeward he did come.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: A drum, a drum!

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Macbeth doth come.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: The weird sisters, hand in hand,

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Posters of the sea and land,

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Thus do go about, about:

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Thrice to thine and thrice to mine

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: And thrice again, to make up nine.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Peace! the charm’s wound up.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: So foul and fair a day I have not seen.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: How far is’t call’d to Forres? What are these

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: So wither’d and so wild in their attire,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That look not like the inhabitants o’ the earth,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And yet are on’t? Live you? or are you aught

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That man may question? You seem to understand me,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: By each at once her choppy finger laying

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Upon her skinny lips: you should be women,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And yet your beards forbid me to interpret

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That you are so.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Speak, if you can: what are you?

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: All hail, Macbeth! hail to thee, thane of Glamis!

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: All hail, Macbeth, hail to thee, thane of Cawdor!

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: All hail, Macbeth, thou shalt be king hereafter!

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Good sir, why do you start; and seem to fear

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Things that do sound so fair? I’ the name of truth,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Are ye fantastical, or that indeed

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Which outwardly ye show? My noble partner

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: You greet with present grace and great prediction

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Of noble having and of royal hope,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That he seems rapt withal: to me you speak not.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: If you can look into the seeds of time,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And say which grain will grow and which will not,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Speak then to me, who neither beg nor fear

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Your favours nor your hate.

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Hail!

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Hail!

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Hail!

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Lesser than Macbeth, and greater.

lines.speaker: SECOND WITCH
lines.text: Not so happy, yet much happier.

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: Thou shalt get kings, though thou be none:

lines.speaker: THIRD WITCH
lines.text: So all hail, Macbeth and Banquo!

lines.speaker: FIRST WITCH
lines.text: Banquo and Macbeth, all hail!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Stay, you imperfect speakers, tell me more:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: By Sinel’s death I know I am thane of Glamis;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But how of Cawdor? the thane of Cawdor lives,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: A prosperous gentleman; and to be king

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Stands not within the prospect of belief,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: No more than to be Cawdor. Say from whence

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: You owe this strange intelligence? or why

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Upon this blasted heath you stop our way

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: With such prophetic greeting? Speak, I charge you. Witches vanish.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: The earth hath bubbles, as the water has,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And these are of them. Whither are they vanish’d?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Into the air; and what seem’d corporal melted

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As breath into the wind. Would they had stay’d!

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Were such things here as we do speak about?

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Or have we eaten on the insane root

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That takes the reason prisoner?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Your children shall be kings.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: You shall be king.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And thane of Cawdor too: went it not so?

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: To the selfsame tune and words. Who’s here?

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: The king hath happily received, Macbeth,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: The news of thy success; and when he reads

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Thy personal venture in the rebels’ fight,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: His wonders and his praises do contend

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Which should be thine or his: silenced with that,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: In viewing o’er the rest o’ the selfsame day,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: He finds thee in the stout Norweyan ranks,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Nothing afeard of what thyself didst make,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Strange images of death. As thick as hail

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Came post with post; and every one did bear

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: Thy praises in his kingdom’s great defence,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: And pour’d them down before him.

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: We are sent

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: To give thee from our royal master thanks;

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Only to herald thee into his sight,

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Not pay thee.

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: And, for an earnest of a greater honour,

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: He bade me, from him, call thee thane of Cawdor:

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: In which addition, hail, most worthy thane!

lines.speaker: ROSS
lines.text: For it is thine.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: What, can the devil speak true?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The thane of Cawdor lives: why do you dress me

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: In borrow’d robes?

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Who was the thane lives yet;

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: But under heavy judgment bears that life

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Which he deserves to lose. Whether he was combined

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: With those of Norway, or did line the rebel

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: With hidden help and vantage, or that with both

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: He labour’d in his country’s wreck, I know not;

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: But treasons capital, confess’d and proved,

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Have overthrown him.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Glamis, and thane of Cawdor!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The greatest is behind. To Ross and Angus. Thanks for your pains.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To Banquo. Do you not hope your children shall be kings,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: When those that gave the thane of Cawdor to me

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Promised no less to them?

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: That trusted home

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Might yet enkindle you unto the crown,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Besides the thane of Cawdor. But ’tis strange:

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And oftentimes, to win us to our harm,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: The instruments of darkness tell us truths,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Win us with honest trifles, to betray’s

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: In deepest consequence.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Cousins, a word, I pray you.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Two truths are told,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As happy prologues to the swelling act

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Of the imperial theme.—I thank you, gentlemen.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: This supernatural soliciting

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Cannot be ill, cannot be good: if ill,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Why hath it given me earnest of success,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Commencing in a truth? I am thane of Cawdor:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If good, why do I yield to that suggestion

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whose horrid image doth unfix my hair

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And make my seated heart knock at my ribs,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Against the use of nature? Present fears

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Are less than horrible imaginings:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: My thought, whose murder yet is but fantastical,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Shakes so my single state of man that function

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Is smother’d in surmise, and nothing is

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But what is not.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Look, how our partner’s rapt.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If chance will have me king, why, chance may crown me,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Without my stir.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: New honours come upon him,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Like our strange garments, cleave not to their mould

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: But with the aid of use.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Come what come may,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Time and the hour runs through the roughest day.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Worthy Macbeth, we stay upon your leisure.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Give me your favour: my dull brain was wrought

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: With things forgotten. Kind gentlemen, your pains

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Are register’d where every day I turn

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The leaf to read them. Let us toward the king.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Think upon what hath chanced, and, at more time,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The interim having weigh’d it, let us speak

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Our free hearts each to other.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Very gladly.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till then, enough. Come, friends.

annotations.title: Weird: not strange, but wyrd
annotations.body:
The modern English 'weird' — meaning odd or uncanny — is a sense the word only fully acquired in the 19th century, and largely because of this play. In 1606, 'weird' carried its Old English root 'wyrd': fate, destiny, the unfolding of what must be. The Norns of Norse mythology, spinners of fate, are often translated as the Weird Sisters.

This matters for the play's metaphysics. If the Sisters are *weird* in the modern sense, they are merely odd witches. If they are *wyrd*, they are agents of fate — not causes of the murder, but its annunciators. The difference determines whether *Macbeth* is a play about what Macbeth chose to do or about what he was always going to do. Shakespeare's text refuses to settle the question; the word carries both possibilities at once, and the production must choose.

Scholarly editors (Braunmuller's New Cambridge, Kerrigan, Clark & Mason) all flag this. When Banquo calls them 'weird sisters' (and later 'imperfect speakers'), he is invoking the Fates of classical myth, not hedge-magic.

annotations.title: Banquo and Macbeth: two reactions, one prophecy
annotations.body:
The prophecy arrives in the same words to both men. Their reactions differ completely. Macbeth is 'rapt withal' — absorbed, silent, visibly shaken. Banquo, seeing this, asks the Sisters for his own future and takes their words as information to be examined, not commands to be obeyed.

Shakespeare's staging here is moral: two men hear the same temptation and react differently. Macbeth's silence is already the murder; he has accepted the future the Sisters describe. Banquo's questions are the defense. The scene demonstrates that the prophecy does not cause the tragedy; it reveals which men will make the tragedy happen.

Compare the Delphic oracle in *Oedipus Rex*: the oracle tells Oedipus he will kill his father, and his attempt to escape it enacts it. In *Macbeth* the Sisters tell a truth, and Macbeth makes it true through action. The difference between Greek fate and Shakespearean fate is the difference between the inevitable and the chosen.
sources: Harold Bloom, *Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human* (1998)
Paul A. Kottman, *Tragic Conditions in Shakespeare* (2009)

annotations.title: 'If chance will have me king, why, chance may crown me'
annotations.body:
Macbeth's first soliloquy after the prophecy is a masterwork of self-deception. He tells himself fate will act for him — 'If chance will have me king, why, chance may crown me, / Without my stir.' The phrase 'without my stir' is the key: Macbeth is asking whether he can have the crown without doing anything for it. The soliloquy lets us see him already considering that he might have to 'stir.'

The language is already the language of the later soliloquies. 'Horrid image,' 'seated heart knock at my ribs,' 'single state of man' — these are the images of 1.7 and 2.1, already present in miniature. Macbeth is not slowly corrupted by Iago-figures; he is visibly already there, thirty lines after meeting the Sisters.

Notice the syntactical breakage. His speech in 1.2 (as reported) was coherent, military, direct. Here it fragments under the weight of the imagined deed. The play will watch this syntax continue to crack.

trials.prompt: When Shakespeare calls them 'weird sisters,' what does the word 'weird' mean in 1606?
options: Strange, odd, or uncanny (the modern sense)
From Old English 'wyrd' — fate, destiny; they are the Fates, not hedge-witches
An invented Shakespeare word with no root
A technical term for Scottish witchcraft

trials.prompt: How do Macbeth's and Banquo's responses to the prophecy differ?
options: They respond identically with caution
Macbeth goes silent and absorbed; Banquo questions the Sisters and examines the news rationally
Macbeth laughs; Banquo weeps
Both ignore the Sisters entirely

trials.prompt: Shakespeare's 'weird sisters' differ from Greek fate in that:
options: They have no causal power
The Sisters tell truths; Macbeth must act to make them true — fate is declared, choice makes it real
They act physically on their victims
They cannot see the future accurately

trials.prompt: 'If chance will have me king, why, chance may crown me, / Without my stir' reveals Macbeth:
options: Is innocent of murderous intent
Is already considering that he might have to 'stir' — that is, act
Trusts God entirely
Wants to abdicate

trials.prompt: What three prophecies do the Sisters deliver to Macbeth?
options: King, thane of Cawdor, thane of Glamis
Thane of Glamis, thane of Cawdor, king hereafter
Earl, king, emperor
Soldier, father, king
